content.body: An ecosystem is a community of living organisms interacting with each other and their non-living environment. Biodiversity refers to the variety of life in an area.

Ecological levels:
Individual → Population → Community → Ecosystem → Biome → Biosphere

Biotic factors: living components (predators, prey, competitors, parasites)
Abiotic factors: non-living (temperature, pH, light, humidity, salinity, soil type)

Niche: the role/position of a species in its ecosystem (how it lives, what it eats, when it is active)

content.title: Uganda's Ecosystems
content.body:
Savanna grassland: Acacia trees, lions, elephants, wildebeest
Tropical rainforest: Bwindi (gorillas), high biodiversity
Wetlands: Lake Victoria shore, papyrus, fishing
Mountain: Rwenzori, alpine vegetation
Lake ecosystems: Lake Victoria, Lake Albert — fish, hippos, crocodiles

content.title: Measuring Biodiversity
content.body:
Species richness: number of different species in an area
Species evenness: how equal the population sizes are
Sampling methods:
- Quadrats: count species in random squares (plants, slow animals)
- Transects: survey along a line (zonation studies)
- Mark-recapture: estimate mobile animal populations

questions.question: A community in ecology is
options: all living organisms in an area
questions.answer: all living organisms in an area
questions.explanation: Community: all populations of different species living and interacting in the same area

questions.question: An ecological niche refers to
options: the role/function of species in ecosystem including what it eats and does
questions.answer: the role/function of species in ecosystem including what it eats and does
questions.explanation: Niche = how a species 'makes its living': diet, habitat use, time active, interactions

questions.question: Abiotic factors in an ecosystem are
options: non-living physical/chemical factors like temperature, pH, light
questions.answer: non-living physical/chemical factors like temperature, pH, light
questions.explanation: Abiotic = non-living. Includes: temperature, rainfall, pH, salinity, light intensity, wind

questions.question: Biodiversity is important because
options: it provides ecosystem stability, food, medicines, resources
questions.answer: it provides ecosystem stability, food, medicines, resources
questions.explanation: High biodiversity = resilient ecosystems, genetic resources, medicines, food security

questions.question: Quadrats are used to sample
options: stationary organisms like plants or slow invertebrates
questions.answer: stationary organisms like plants or slow invertebrates
questions.explanation: Quadrats work for organisms that don't move away: plants, fungi, barnacles, slow invertebrates

questions.question: Mark-recapture estimates population size. Formula: N = M×C/R where M=marked, C=captured second time, R=recaptured marked. If M=50, C=80, R=20, N =
A keystone species is one that
options: has a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem relative to its abundance
is at top of food chain
questions.answer: has a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem relative to its abundance
questions.explanation: Removing a keystone species causes dramatic ecosystem changes (e.g. elephants in savanna, sea otters)

questions.question: Biome is
options: large-scale terrestrial/aquatic zone with characteristic climate and organisms
questions.answer: large-scale terrestrial/aquatic zone with characteristic climate and organisms
questions.explanation: Biomes: tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, tundra, taiga, temperate forest, ocean

questions.question: Primary succession begins on
questions.answer: bare rock or new land
questions.explanation: Primary succession: colonisation of bare, lifeless substrate (lava, sand dune, glacier retreat)

questions.question: Transect sampling is best for studying
questions.answer: zonation/gradual change across an area
questions.explanation: Transects sample along a line revealing how communities change across an environmental gradient

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Human activities significantly impact ecosystems, often negatively. Understanding these impacts is crucial for conservation and sustainable development.

content.title: Deforestation
content.body:
Causes: agriculture, logging, settlement, charcoal
Consequences:
- Loss of biodiversity (habitat destruction)
- Increased CO₂ (less carbon fixation, burning releases CO₂)
- Soil erosion (no roots to hold soil)
- Flooding (no interception)
- Climate change
- Water cycle disruption

content.title: Pollution
content.body:
Water pollution: agricultural runoff (nitrates/phosphates → eutrophication), industrial effluent, sewage
Eutrophication: excess nutrients → algal bloom → blocks light → aquatic plants die → bacteria decompose → oxygen depleted → fish die
Air pollution: SO₂ (acid rain), CO₂ (global warming), particulates (respiratory disease)
Soil pollution: pesticides, heavy metals, plastics

content.title: Conservation Methods
content.body:
In situ: protection in natural habitat (national parks, game reserves, marine parks)
Ex situ: outside natural habitat (zoos, seed banks, botanical gardens)
Sustainable use: fishing quotas, selective logging, ecotourism
Reforestation/restoration, captive breeding programs

questions.question: Eutrophication is caused by
options: excess nutrients (nitrates/phosphates) causing algal bloom and O₂ depletion
questions.answer: excess nutrients (nitrates/phosphates) causing algal bloom and O₂ depletion
questions.explanation: Eutrophication sequence: nutrients from farm runoff → algal bloom → light blocked → plants die → decomposition depletes O₂ → fish die

questions.question: Deforestation increases atmospheric CO₂ because
options: burning releases CO₂ and fewer trees fix CO₂
questions.answer: burning releases CO₂ and fewer trees fix CO₂
questions.explanation: Trees are carbon sinks; burning releases stored carbon. Fewer trees = less CO₂ absorption

questions.question: Acid rain is caused mainly by
options: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.answer: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.explanation: SO₂+H₂O→H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid); NOₓ+H₂O→HNO₃. These fall as acid rain

questions.question: In situ conservation means
options: protection of species in their natural habitat
questions.answer: protection of species in their natural habitat
questions.explanation: In situ: species protected where they naturally live (national parks, nature reserves)

questions.question: The greenhouse effect is enhanced by
options: increased CO₂, CH₄, N₂O from human activities trapping more heat
questions.answer: increased CO₂, CH₄, N₂O from human activities trapping more heat
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases absorb outgoing infrared radiation, warming Earth — enhanced by human emissions

questions.question: Lake Victoria has suffered biodiversity loss mainly due to
options: introduction of Nile perch and water hyacinth, plus eutrophication
questions.answer: introduction of Nile perch and water hyacinth, plus eutrophication
questions.explanation: Nile perch ate native cichlids (hundreds of extinctions); water hyacinth outcompetes and deoxygenates water

questions.question: Sustainable development means
options: meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs
questions.answer: meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs
questions.explanation: Brundtland Commission definition: sustainability balances development with resource preservation for the future

questions.question: Bioaccumulation in food chains means
options: toxic substances like DDT concentrate in organisms up the food chain
questions.answer: toxic substances like DDT concentrate in organisms up the food chain
questions.explanation: Fat-soluble toxins (DDT, mercury) accumulate in tissue; higher predators accumulate most (biomagnification)

questions.question: Soil erosion increases after deforestation because
options: tree roots no longer hold soil particles, and rain washes soil away
questions.answer: tree roots no longer hold soil particles, and rain washes soil away
questions.explanation: Tree roots bind soil; leaf canopy intercepts rain. Without trees, rain impact and runoff wash away topsoil

questions.question: Ex situ conservation includes
questions.answer: seed banks and zoos
questions.explanation: Ex situ: conservation outside natural habitat — zoos (animals), seed banks (plants), botanic gardens

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Ecology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Ecology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
